name: IPCC Overview
description:
A finished cause-and-effect mural of global warming: human activities → emissions → concentrations and sinks → energy imbalance → climate responses → impacts → responses.

Click any card for an explanation and sources. This is a browseable explainer, not a workshop.
credit: Pedagogical layout inspired by La Fresque du Climat / Climate Fresk (Cédric Ringenbach). Card text and links are original and based on public IPCC AR6 findings—not a copy of the official deck.
disclaimer: Not an official Climate Fresk / Fresque du Climat product. Official Fresk materials are CC BY-NC-ND and must not be copied. For a facilitated workshop, see climatefresk.org. Figures here are order-of-magnitude educational summaries—consult the cited IPCC chapters for authoritative wording.

cards.label: Fossil energy
cards.summary: Coal, oil and gas burned for electricity, heat and fuel
cards.details: Most anthropogenic CO₂ comes from burning fossil carbon that was stored underground. Electricity & heat, industry fuel use, and transport dominate global fossil CO₂.
cards.key_figures: Fossil CO₂ is the largest share of anthropogenic GHG emissions (IPCC AR6).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Industry
cards.summary: Process emissions and energy use in materials and manufacturing
cards.details: Cement, steel, chemicals and other industry emit both from fuel combustion and from chemical processes (e.g. calcination of limestone for cement).
cards.key_figures: Industry is among the largest emitting sectors globally alongside energy supply and AFOLU.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers
sources.note: Industry mitigation chapter context in WG3

cards.label: Transport
cards.summary: Road, aviation, shipping and rail fossil fuel use
cards.details: Transport emissions are dominated by oil products. Road vehicles are the largest slice; aviation and shipping grow with trade and travel.
cards.key_figures: Transport relies heavily on liquid fossil fuels; electrification and efficiency are key levers (WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Buildings
cards.summary: Heating, cooling, cooking and electricity in homes and workplaces
cards.details: Buildings use energy directly (gas, oil, biomass) and indirectly via electricity. Envelope efficiency and heat pumps cut demand and emissions.
cards.key_figures: Building energy demand depends strongly on climate, insulation, and fuel mix.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Deforestation & land use
cards.summary: Clearing forests and converting ecosystems releases stored carbon
cards.details: When forests or peatlands are cleared or degraded, carbon stored in biomass and soils enters the atmosphere as CO₂ (and sometimes CH₄/N₂O from fires and soils).
cards.key_figures: AFOLU (agriculture, forestry and other land use) is a major emissions category and also hosts sinks.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Agriculture & livestock
cards.summary: Methane from ruminants and rice; nitrous oxide from fertilizers
cards.details: Enteric fermentation, manure, flooded rice, and nitrogen fertilizers drive non-CO₂ GHGs. Food systems also link to land-use change.
cards.key_figures: CH₄ and N₂O from AFOLU are a large share of non-CO₂ anthropogenic forcing.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 6 — Short-lived climate forcers
IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: CO₂ emissions
cards.summary: Carbon dioxide from fossil fuels and land-use change
cards.details: CO₂ is long-lived in the Earth system. Cumulative CO₂ emissions tightly constrain long-term warming. About half of emitted CO₂ stays in the atmosphere on multi-decadal timescales; the rest is taken up by land and ocean sinks.
cards.key_figures: Each 1000 GtCO₂ of cumulative emissions is associated with ~0.45°C (best estimate range in AR6) of warming (TCRE).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle

cards.label: CH₄ emissions
cards.summary: Methane from fossil fuels, livestock, waste and wetlands disturbance
cards.details: Methane is a potent but shorter-lived GHG. Cutting CH₄ reduces near-term warming quickly compared with CO₂ alone.
cards.key_figures: CH₄ has a high Global Warming Potential over 20–100 years but a much shorter atmospheric lifetime than CO₂.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 6 — Short-lived climate forcers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: N₂O emissions
cards.summary: Nitrous oxide mainly from agriculture and some industry
cards.details: N₂O is long-lived and also depletes stratospheric ozone. Agricultural soils are the dominant anthropogenic source.
cards.key_figures: N₂O is the third major long-lived GHG after CO₂ and CH₄.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 6 — Short-lived climate forcers

cards.label: Aerosols & air pollution
cards.summary: Particles that cool (and some that warm) and harm health
cards.details: Sulfate and other aerosols from burning fossil fuels and biomass reflect sunlight and modify clouds, masking some greenhouse warming. Cleaning air pollution reduces that mask while improving health.
cards.key_figures: Aerosol cooling is a major uncertainty in the historical energy budget (WG1 Ch7).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 6 — Short-lived climate forcers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 7 — Energy budget & radiative forcing

cards.label: GHG concentrations
cards.summary: Rising atmospheric CO₂, CH₄ and N₂O
cards.details: Emissions accumulate in the atmosphere when they exceed natural sinks. CO₂ is now higher than at any time in at least 2 million years (AR6).
cards.key_figures: CO₂ ≈ 410+ ppm in recent observations cited in AR6 (levels continue to rise).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle

cards.label: Land carbon sink
cards.summary: Forests and soils absorb part of emitted CO₂
cards.details: Photosynthesis and soil processes remove a substantial fraction of anthropogenic CO₂ each year. The sink is climate- and land-use-sensitive and is not unlimited.
cards.key_figures: Land and ocean together have taken up roughly half of anthropogenic CO₂ emissions historically (order of magnitude; WG1 Ch5).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle

cards.label: Ocean CO₂ uptake
cards.summary: Oceans dissolve CO₂ and store heat and carbon
cards.details: Ocean uptake slows atmospheric CO₂ rise but causes ocean acidification and is rate-limited by circulation and chemistry.
cards.key_figures: The ocean has absorbed a large share of excess heat and a substantial fraction of anthropogenic CO₂ (WG1 Ch5, Ch9).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean, cryosphere, sea level

cards.label: Greenhouse effect
cards.summary: GHGs trap outgoing infrared radiation
cards.details: The natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth habitable. Extra GHGs from human activities strengthen the effect, reducing the efficiency of outgoing longwave radiation to space until the planet warms enough to restore energy balance.
cards.key_figures: Without the natural greenhouse effect, global mean surface temperature would be far below freezing.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 7 — Energy budget & radiative forcing
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Radiative forcing
cards.summary: Net change in Earth’s energy balance from drivers
cards.details: Positive forcing (GHGs) warms; negative forcing (many aerosols) cools. Effective radiative forcing (ERF) is the AR6 metric linking drivers to the energy imbalance.
cards.key_figures: Well-mixed GHGs provide the largest positive ERF since 1750; aerosols provide a substantial negative ERF (WG1 Ch7).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 7 — Energy budget & radiative forcing
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Earth energy imbalance
cards.summary: More energy in than out — the planet is heating
cards.details: When incoming absorbed solar exceeds outgoing longwave, heat accumulates—mostly in the ocean. The imbalance is the most direct measure that warming is ongoing.
cards.key_figures: Most excess heat (~90%) goes into the ocean (WG1).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 7 — Energy budget & radiative forcing
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean, cryosphere, sea level

cards.label: Global warming
cards.summary: Rising global mean surface temperature
cards.details: Observed warming since the late 19th century is unequivocal and human-caused. Near-term and long-term warming depend on cumulative emissions and climate sensitivity.
cards.key_figures: AR6: human influence has warmed the climate at a rate unprecedented in at least 2000 years; ~1.1°C warmer in 2011–2020 vs 1850–1900 (SPM).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Ocean heat content
cards.summary: Oceans store most of the excess heat
cards.details: Ocean warming drives thermal expansion (sea level), intensifies marine heatwaves, and affects circulation and ecosystems.
cards.key_figures: Ocean heat content has increased through the observational record (WG1 Ch9).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean, cryosphere, sea level

cards.label: Ice melt
cards.summary: Glaciers, ice sheets and sea ice lose mass
cards.details: Mountain glaciers and the Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets contribute to sea-level rise. Sea-ice loss does not raise sea level directly but amplifies warming via albedo feedback.
cards.key_figures: Ice sheet and glacier mass loss has accelerated (WG1 Ch9).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean, cryosphere, sea level

cards.label: Sea-level rise
cards.summary: Thermal expansion + land ice melt raise coasts’ risk
cards.details: Global mean sea level is rising and will continue for centuries even if warming stabilizes, because ice sheets and deep ocean heat adjust slowly.
cards.key_figures: Global mean sea level rose ~0.20 m over 1901–2018 (WG1 SPM order of magnitude).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 9 — Ocean, cryosphere, sea level
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Water cycle changes
cards.summary: A warmer atmosphere holds more moisture; rainfall patterns shift
cards.details: Intensification of the water cycle means heavier extremes in many regions, altered drought risk, and monsoon changes—regionally complex.
cards.key_figures: AR6 finds the water cycle has intensified with warming (Ch8).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 8 — Water cycle

cards.label: Climate extremes
cards.summary: Heatwaves, heavy rain, droughts and fire weather intensify
cards.details: Attribution science links many observed changes in extremes to human-caused warming. Risks compound when extremes hit vulnerable systems.
cards.key_figures: Hot extremes have become more frequent and intense; heavy precipitation has increased in many regions (WG1 Ch11).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 11 — Weather and climate extremes
IPCC AR6 WG1 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Ecosystem stress
cards.summary: Biodiversity loss, coral bleaching, shifting biomes
cards.details: Warming, extremes, acidification and land-use change stress ecosystems. Some systems approach thresholds (e.g. warm-water corals).
cards.key_figures: WG2 assesses widespread adverse impacts already underway across ecosystems.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Food & water security
cards.summary: Yields, fisheries and freshwater under pressure
cards.details: Climate hazards interact with soil degradation, overuse and inequality. Impacts are uneven—often worst where adaptive capacity is lowest.
cards.key_figures: Climate change has already affected food security in several regions (WG2 SPM).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers

cards.label: Human health
cards.summary: Heat stress, disease vectors, air quality and mental health
cards.details: Heat extremes raise mortality and morbidity; climate shifts alter infectious disease risk; wildfire smoke and ozone worsen air quality.
cards.key_figures: Health risks rise with every increment of warming, with strong equity dimensions (WG2).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Livelihoods & displacement
cards.summary: Economic losses, migration pressures and conflict risks
cards.details: Climate impacts cascade through infrastructure, labor productivity and coasts. Outcomes depend heavily on governance and vulnerability—not climate alone.
cards.key_figures: WG2 emphasizes cascading and compounding risks across societies.

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Mitigation
cards.summary: Cut emissions: clean energy, efficiency, land, demand
cards.details: Deep, rapid GHG reductions are required to limit warming. Pathways combine electrification, renewables, efficiency, methane cuts, and protecting/enhancing sinks—while managing equity.
cards.key_figures: Limiting warming to 1.5°C with limited overshoot requires global GHG emissions to peak ASAP and fall sharply this decade (SYR / WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Adaptation
cards.summary: Reduce harm: early warning, resilient infrastructure, social protection
cards.details: Adaptation lowers risks but cannot prevent all losses—especially at higher warming. Effective adaptation is place-specific and needs finance and institutions.
cards.key_figures: Adaptation progress is underway but gaps remain; limits to adaptation exist (WG2).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG2 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report

cards.label: Protect & enhance sinks
cards.summary: Ecosystems and land management that store carbon
cards.details: Halting deforestation, restoring ecosystems, and improving soils can increase removals—but sinks cannot substitute for fossil CO₂ cuts at the needed scale.
cards.key_figures: Land-based mitigation has cobenefits and constraints (food, biodiversity) (WG3).

sources.label: IPCC AR6 WG3 Summary for Policymakers
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 5 — Global Carbon Cycle
